top_papers.title: LookaheadKV: Fast and Accurate KV Cache Eviction by Glimpsing into the Future without Generation
top_papers.summary: Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) rely on key-value (KV) caching to avoid redundant computation during autoregressive inference. While this mechanism greatly improves efficiency, the cache size grows linearly with the input sequence length, quickly becoming a bottleneck for long-context tasks. Existing solutions mitigate this problem by evicting prompt KV that are deemed unimportant, guided by estimated importance scores. Notably, a recent line of work proposes to improve eviction quality by "glimpsing into the future", in which a draft generator produces a surrogate future response approximating the target model's true response, and this surrogate is subsequently used to estimate the importance of cached KV more accurately. However, these approaches rely on computationally expensive draft generation, which introduces substantial prefilling overhead and limits their practicality in real-world deployment. To address this challenge, we propose LookaheadKV, a lightweight eviction framework that leverages the strength of surrogate future response without requiring explicit draft generation. LookaheadKV augments transformer layers with parameter-efficient modules trained to predict true importance scores with high accuracy. Our design ensures negligible runtime overhead comparable to existing inexpensive heuristics, while achieving accuracy superior to more costly approximation methods. Extensive experiments on long-context understanding benchmarks, across a wide range of models, demonstrate that our method not only outperforms recent competitive baselines in various long-context understanding tasks, but also reduces the eviction cost by up to 14.5x, leading to significantly faster time-to-first-token. Our code is available at https://github.com/SamsungLabs/LookaheadKV.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper replaces draft-generation-based future-aware KV eviction with a learned importance predictor, aiming to keep eviction cheap while preserving long-context accuracy.
ai.summary_zh: KV cache eviction matters because long-context autoregressive inference makes cache memory grow linearly, and prior "glimpse into the future" methods improve eviction quality but add expensive draft generation during prefilling. This paper proposes LookaheadKV, which augments transformer layers with parameter-efficient modules trained to predict true KV importance scores without explicitly generating surrogate responses. The abstract claims this keeps runtime overhead near inexpensive heuristics while outperforming stronger but costlier approximation baselines on long-context understanding benchmarks across multiple models. It also reports up to 14.5x lower eviction cost and faster time-to-first-token, making the work directly relevant to serving efficiency. The main missing details are how the modules are trained and integrated, what extra per-layer overhead they introduce, and how fair the hardware and baseline comparisons are.
ai.background_zh: Long-context LLM serving depends on KV caching, but cache growth creates a memory and latency bottleneck, so better eviction policies have become a core inference-systems problem.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to get the accuracy benefit of future-aware KV eviction without paying the substantial prefilling overhead of explicit draft generation.
ai.approach_zh: LookaheadKV adds parameter-efficient modules to transformer layers and trains them to predict true KV importance scores using future-aware signals without running a draft generator at inference time.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract says experiments on long-context understanding benchmarks across multiple models outperform recent baselines and reduce eviction cost by up to 14.5x with faster time-to-first-token, but it does not make the hardware setup or comparison protocol clear.
ai.value_zh: If the reported tradeoff holds, this is a practical serving-side method for reducing KV-management overhead while keeping long-context quality higher than cheap heuristic eviction.
open_questions: How are the added modules trained, and do they require model-specific supervision or future-response labels that are expensive to obtain?
What is the exact inference-time overhead in parameters, activations, and memory traffic for the added layer modules?
Which cache budgets, baselines, hardware settings, and TTFT measurement rules are used, and are end-to-end comparisons to draft-generation methods fair?
core_contributions: Removes explicit draft generation from future-aware KV eviction while keeping the core idea of using future information.
Introduces a learned, parameter-efficient mechanism inside transformer layers to predict KV importance scores.
Reports better long-context benchmark performance together with much lower eviction cost and faster TTFT than recent baselines.
why_read: It targets a real deployment bottleneck: KV cache pressure in long-context inference.
The source of the claimed speedup is clear and systems-relevant: less eviction-time work than draft-based lookahead methods.
It is a useful case study in learned serving policy design rather than pure model-side modification.
risks: The abstract does not specify the training cost, training data, or whether the method must be retrained for each base model.
Runtime gains may depend heavily on hidden assumptions about hardware, cache budget, batch size, or sequence-length regime.
recommended_for: Researchers working on LLM inference systems and long-context serving
Engineers optimizing KV cache management and time-to-first-token
Teams comparing heuristic versus learned cache eviction policies
ai.reading_priority_reason: High because it directly matches the highest-priority LLM inference systems focus and the abstract reports concrete serving-side gains in eviction cost and TTFT, with a very strong recommendation score signal.
ai.fallback_summary: Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) rely on key-value (KV) caching to avoid redundant computation during autoregressive inference.

top_papers.title: AgentServe: Algorithm-System Co-Design for Efficient Agentic AI Serving on a Consumer-Grade GPU
top_papers.summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as AI agents that operate in short reasoning-action loops, interleaving model computation with external calls. Unlike traditional chat applications, these agentic workloads require inference serving systems to balance low latency, stable token emission, and throughput under multiple request arrivals from different AI agents. Recent deployments highlight a shift toward running small language models (SLMs) locally on consumer-grade GPUs, driven by privacy, compliance, and cost constraints. When heterogeneous requests overlap on a single GPU, long prefills and short decodes contend for resources, creating head-of-line blocking that destabilizes interactive performance. By analyzing agent workloads, we observe that their execution naturally separates into cold prefills, which process long system prompts, resume prefills, which append tool outputs to cached contexts, and short decodes, which are latency-critical. This mix intensifies contention compared to conventional chatbot serving. We present AgentServe, a single-GPU serving system that ensures stable multi-agent execution under such conditions by isolating prefills from decodes, applying dynamic budgeting to resume prefills, and allocating GPU resources through pre-established CUDA Green Context slots with adaptive control. Evaluation results show that AgentServe significantly improves latency stability while sustaining competitive throughput, achieving up to 2.8x TTFT improvement and 2.7x TPOT improvement over state-of-the-art baselines across different settings.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper presents a single-GPU serving system for agentic LLM workloads that separates long and short phases of execution to reduce latency instability under overlapping requests.
ai.summary_zh: The paper targets a serving problem that is becoming more important as small language models run locally as agents on consumer GPUs and interleave decoding with tool calls. It argues that agent workloads create a harder scheduling mix than standard chat because cold prefills, resume prefills, and short latency-critical decodes contend on one GPU and cause head-of-line blocking. AgentServe addresses this with prefill/decode isolation, dynamic budgeting for resume prefills, and GPU allocation through pre-established CUDA Green Context slots with adaptive control. The abstract claims improved latency stability with competitive throughput, including up to 2.8x better TTFT and 2.7x better TPOT than state-of-the-art baselines across multiple settings. The main missing detail is how these gains depend on the exact GPU, workload mix, baseline configuration, and the overheads of the proposed resource partitioning.
ai.background_zh: Agent-style LLM serving differs from ordinary chat serving because requests repeatedly switch between model execution and external actions, which makes single-GPU scheduling more bursty and interference-prone.
ai.problem_zh: The core problem is how to keep token latency stable while preserving throughput when heterogeneous prefill and decode phases from multiple agents overlap on a consumer-grade GPU.
ai.approach_zh: The system decomposes agent requests into cold prefills, resume prefills, and short decodes, then isolates prefills from decodes, budgets resume prefills dynamically, and assigns GPU resources with adaptive CUDA Green Context slot control.
ai.evidence_zh: According to the abstract, experiments across different settings show better latency stability with competitive throughput and report up to 2.8x TTFT improvement and 2.7x TPOT improvement over state-of-the-art baselines, but the abstract does not make the evaluation setup or fairness details clear.
ai.value_zh: If the method holds up, it offers a practical serving design for local multi-agent execution on modest hardware where interactive latency matters more than maximizing raw batch throughput.
open_questions: What GPU model, model sizes, agent traces, and request concurrency levels were used, and do the reported gains persist outside those settings?
How are the state-of-the-art baselines configured, and is the comparison fair on batching, KV-cache handling, and scheduling policy?
What overheads or utilization losses come from CUDA Green Context slot partitioning and dynamic resume-prefill budgeting under lighter or more decode-heavy workloads?
core_contributions: It identifies agent serving on a single GPU as a distinct scheduling regime with cold prefills, resume prefills, and short decodes rather than a standard chat-style request pattern.
It proposes a serving design that explicitly separates prefills from decodes and treats resume prefills as a budgeted middle class of work.
It uses pre-established CUDA Green Context slots with adaptive control as the resource-allocation mechanism for stable multi-agent execution on consumer hardware.
why_read: It is directly about LLM inference serving efficiency rather than generic agent orchestration, with concrete focus on TTFT/TPOT behavior under contention.
It frames agentic workloads as a systems scheduling problem and gives a mechanism-level answer instead of only reporting end-to-end latency numbers.
It may be useful for local or edge deployments where a single consumer GPU must multiplex several agent sessions without severe interactive jitter.
risks: The abstract does not clarify whether the gains come from a broadly applicable scheduling idea or from workload-specific tuning.
CUDA Green Context dependence may limit portability or make the design sensitive to specific driver, runtime, or GPU assumptions that are not stated here.
recommended_for: Researchers working on LLM serving schedulers for mixed prefill/decode workloads.
Engineers building local or edge multi-agent systems on a single consumer GPU.
Readers interested in runtime and resource-allocation co-design for interactive inference.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is a strong fit for LLM inference systems work because it focuses on single-GPU serving, latency stability, and scheduling under mixed agent workloads, and the abstract includes concrete mechanism and outcome claims rather than only motivation.
ai.fallback_summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as AI agents that operate in short reasoning-action loops, interleaving model computation with external calls.

top_papers.title: Towards Cold-Start Drafting and Continual Refining: A Value-Driven Memory Approach with Application to NPU Kernel Synthesis
top_papers.summary: Deploying Large Language Models to data-scarce programming domains poses significant challenges, particularly for kernel synthesis on emerging Domain-Specific Architectures where a "Data Wall" limits available training data. While models excel on data-rich platforms like CUDA, they suffer catastrophic performance drops on data-scarce ecosystems such as NPU programming. To overcome this cold-start barrier without expensive fine-tuning, we introduce EvoKernel, a self-evolving agentic framework that automates the lifecycle of kernel synthesis from initial drafting to continual refining. EvoKernel addresses this by formulating the synthesis process as a memory-based reinforcement learning task. Through a novel value-driven retrieval mechanism, it learns stage-specific Q-values that prioritize experiences based on their contribution to the current objective, whether bootstrapping a feasible draft or iteratively refining latency. Furthermore, by enabling cross-task memory sharing, the agent generalizes insights from simple to complex operators. By building an NPU variant of KernelBench and evaluating on it, EvoKernel improves frontier models' correctness from 11.0% to 83.0% and achieves a median speedup of 3.60x over initial drafts through iterative refinement. This demonstrates that value-guided experience accumulation allows general-purpose models to master the kernel synthesis task on niche hardware ecosystems. Our official page is available at https://evokernel.zhuo.li.
top_papers.matched_domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

match_details.domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

ai.one_liner_zh: The paper presents EvoKernel, an agentic memory-based reinforcement learning framework that bootstraps and iteratively refines NPU kernels by retrieving prior experiences with stage-specific value estimates.
ai.summary_zh: Kernel synthesis for emerging NPUs faces a data wall: general-purpose LLMs perform well in CUDA-rich settings but degrade sharply when domain data is scarce. This paper proposes EvoKernel, which treats drafting and refinement as a memory-based reinforcement learning problem and uses stage-specific Q-values to retrieve prior experiences that are most useful for either finding a feasible kernel or reducing latency. It also shares memory across tasks so knowledge from simpler operators can transfer to more complex ones. The abstract reports evaluation on an NPU variant of KernelBench, with correctness improving from 11.0% to 83.0% and a median 3.60x speedup over initial drafts after iterative refinement. The main missing detail is evaluation fidelity: the abstract does not explain the NPU stack, benchmark composition, baseline strength, or how the value estimates are learned and validated.
ai.background_zh: As kernel generation expands beyond CUDA into emerging accelerators, data scarcity becomes a core bottleneck because these ecosystems lack the code volume and training signals that current LLM code models depend on.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to overcome cold-start failure in NPU kernel synthesis without expensive domain fine-tuning, while still improving performance after an initial working draft.
ai.approach_zh: EvoKernel models synthesis as a staged memory-based RL loop, learns stage-specific Q-values to rank prior experiences for drafting versus refinement, and shares memory across related kernel tasks.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports results on an NPU variant of KernelBench, claiming correctness improves from 11.0% to 83.0% and median performance improves by 3.60x over initial drafts, but it does not make the hardware setup, task mix, or baseline protocol clear.
ai.value_zh: If the method is robust, it offers a practical way to adapt general-purpose LLMs to niche accelerator programming by turning prior synthesis attempts into reusable optimization knowledge.
open_questions: How are the stage-specific Q-values defined, trained, and updated during cold-start drafting versus latency refinement?
What exactly is included in the NPU KernelBench variant, and how comparable is its evaluation protocol to existing kernel synthesis benchmarks?
Are the reported gains measured against single-shot drafts only, or against stronger baselines such as repeated sampling, search-based refinement, or other agentic systems on the same hardware?
core_contributions: Frames cold-start kernel synthesis on data-scarce NPUs as a memory-based reinforcement learning problem spanning initial drafting and continual refinement.
Introduces value-driven retrieval with stage-specific Q-values to prioritize past experiences by their usefulness to the current synthesis objective.
Adds cross-task memory sharing and evaluates the approach on an NPU-oriented KernelBench variant with reported correctness and speedup gains.
why_read: It targets a real systems bottleneck: getting LLM-based kernel synthesis to work on emerging accelerators without domain-specific fine-tuning.
The method ties agentic code generation to explicit memory valuation and reuse, which is more interesting than plain prompt-loop refinement.
It may be relevant for teams exploring compiler or runtime co-design for low-data kernel generation on non-CUDA hardware.
risks: The abstract does not specify the NPU hardware, compiler stack, or runtime conditions, so external validity is unclear.
The main comparison is against initial drafts, so baseline fairness versus stronger search or agentic alternatives is not established in the abstract alone.
recommended_for: Researchers working on LLM-driven kernel synthesis for NPUs or other niche accelerators.
Engineers studying agentic refinement loops for low-data code generation workflows.
Compiler and runtime teams interested in memory-guided optimization for generated low-level code.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is a strong fit for LLM code generation and low-level kernel synthesis, and the abstract includes concrete correctness and speedup claims on NPU codegen, even though evaluation fairness and reproducibility still need checking.
ai.fallback_summary: Deploying Large Language Models to data-scarce programming domains poses significant challenges, particularly for kernel synthesis on emerging Domain-Specific Architectures where a "Data Wall" limits available training data.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are training and inference efficiency. The set is anchored by LookaheadKV: Fast and Accurate KV… and AgentServe: Algorithm-System…. Towards Cold-Start Drafting and… adds another data point on whether the same line of work carries stronger evidence or engineering value.
reading_strategy: Start with LookaheadKV: Fast and Accurate KV… and verify whether it defines the training and inference efficiency problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
Then read AgentServe: Algorithm-System… to see whether the novelty comes from model design, training procedure, or evaluation reframing.
Use Towards Cold-Start Drafting and… as a final cross-check on experimental boundaries and practical value.
